# **Architecting Evaluation Frameworks for Enterprise Metadata Retrieval Systems**

## **Introduction to Enterprise Metadata Retrieval Dynamics**

The contemporary enterprise data ecosystem is characterized by an exponential proliferation of disparate analytical assets, ranging from complex relational database tables and multidimensional schemas to analytical dashboards, unstructured documents, and highly specific business metrics. As modern organizations pivot aggressively toward data-centric operating models, the discoverability and governance of these assets—collectively orchestrated by enterprise data catalogs—have become paramount operational necessities.1 Traditional data discovery mechanisms have historically relied on exact-match keyword algorithms or primitive lexical search over highly fragmented metadata repositories.1 However, the advent of sophisticated retrieval-augmented systems and semantic search architectures has fundamentally altered user expectations. Modern data practitioners now demand retrieval engines that natively understand natural language intent, domain-specific corporate vocabularies, and the complex contextual relationships that exist between different data products.3

Evaluating the precise efficacy of a retrieval system operating atop an enterprise metadata index presents a unique set of infrastructural, mathematical, and methodological challenges. Unlike public-facing web search engines or large-scale e-commerce platforms that generate terabytes of interaction logs daily, internal enterprise data catalogs frequently suffer from extreme data sparsity.4 Clickstream data—the traditional foundational bedrock for implicit relevance judgments and advanced learning-to-rank algorithms—is often minimal, highly noisy, or entirely absent in low-volume internal search services.4 Consequently, search relevance engineers cannot rely on historical user behavior to construct the statistically significant evaluation benchmarks required to tune their systems.

Furthermore, the fundamental nature of the indexed documents within a data catalog adds a significant layer of complexity to the evaluation process. The target documents are not cohesive narrative texts but rather highly structured metadata payloads containing schema definitions, technical documentation, business tags, glossary terms, team usage statistics, and popularity scores.6 Assessing whether a specific database table or analytical chart is the most relevant answer to a user's natural language query requires an evaluation framework capable of understanding both semantic alignment and operational utility.7 Semantic alignment dictates whether the metadata description logically matches the user's query, while operational utility dictates whether the asset is the most trusted, frequently used, and authoritative source for that specific domain.

The central objective of this comprehensive analysis is to delineate a highly scalable, mathematically rigorous methodology for evaluating an enterprise metadata retrieval system under the constraints of limited clickstream data. Specifically, the framework will detail the mathematical foundations of two critical evaluation metrics: Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain at 10 (NDCG@10) and Hit Rate at 10 (Hit Rate@10). Crucially, the analysis will provide an exhaustive, step-by-step blueprint for generating the requisite ground-truth evaluation datasets utilizing the resources currently available within the specified architectural constraints. By leveraging advanced Large Language Model (LLM) synthetic query generation, adapting existing natural language question-and-answer (QnA) datasets through BM25 baseline retrieval, and utilizing LLMs as automated, multi-graded relevance judges, organizations can construct rigorous evaluation pipelines that accurately reflect the profound nuances of enterprise data discovery.

## **The Mathematical Anatomy of Search Quality Metrics**

To systematically assess and iteratively improve the performance of a metadata retrieval system, the engineering architecture must rely on standardized, quantifiable metrics that capture entirely different dimensions of search quality. In the context of enterprise data catalogs, a user typically expects the most relevant data assets to appear within the first page of search results, as attention spans degrade rapidly beyond initial impressions. Therefore, the evaluation pipeline is typically constrained to a depth of ![][image1]. The two primary metrics utilized to quantify this performance are Hit Rate@10 and NDCG@10, which serve highly complementary analytical purposes.9 Understanding the mathematical mechanics of these metrics is essential for designing the ground-truth datasets that will ultimately feed them.

### **Hit Rate at 10 (Hit Rate@10)**

Hit Rate, which is also frequently referred to as Success Rate or Recall@k in various information retrieval contexts, is a binary, recall-oriented metric that measures the absolute proportion of queries for which at least one highly relevant document appears anywhere within the top ![][image2] results.9 In the specialized domain of enterprise metadata search, Hit Rate@10 answers a fundamental, baseline operational question: Did the underlying retrieval pipeline successfully surface the required data asset before the user abandoned the search interface entirely?

This metric is particularly valuable for diagnosing catastrophic retrieval failures within a system. Such failures typically occur when the baseline retrieval mechanism entirely misses the necessary vocabulary due to severe lexical mismatches, or when aggressive pre-filtering mechanisms prematurely exclude the correct asset from the candidate pool.10 Hit Rate is intentionally agnostic to the exact position of the relevant document, provided it exists within the designated top\-![][image2] threshold. This makes it an excellent proxy for measuring basic system coverage and the absolute limits of the index's discoverability.

For a defined set of evaluation queries ![][image3], the Hit Rate at a specific depth ![][image2] is defined mathematically by the following formulation:

![][image4]  
In this mathematical representation, ![][image5] represents the ranked list of the top ![][image2] retrieved documents returned by the system for a specific query ![][image6]. The function ![][image7] represents the ground-truth relevance score of a specific document ![][image8] with respect to the query ![][image6]. The variable ![][image9] serves as the minimum relevance threshold required to consider a retrieved document a successful "hit." Finally, ![][image10] represents the indicator function, which evaluates to 1 if the internal condition is met (meaning a relevant document was found), and evaluates to 0 if the condition is false.

While Hit Rate@10 provides an excellent, easily interpretable baseline for system coverage, it is fundamentally insufficient for measuring the optimal user experience or the true quality of a ranking algorithm. An enterprise search system that consistently places the correct analytical dashboard at rank 10 achieves a theoretically perfect Hit Rate@10. However, this system delivers a highly suboptimal user experience compared to a finely-tuned system that consistently places the authoritative asset at rank 1\.12 To capture the quality of the sorting mechanism, the evaluation framework must incorporate a rank-aware metric capable of handling varying degrees of utility.

### **Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain at 10 (NDCG@10)**

To effectively address the structural limitations of position-agnostic metrics like Hit Rate, the evaluation framework must deploy a metric that heavily penalizes systems for burying highly relevant results deep within the result set. Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain (NDCG) serves as the undisputed industry-standard metric for evaluating the quality of ranked lists in complex information retrieval scenarios.12 NDCG operates on two foundational assumptions that align perfectly with human psychological behavior during search tasks. First, it assumes that highly relevant documents are exponentially more useful to the user when they appear higher in the search results. Second, it assumes that documents possess varying, granular degrees of relevance (graded relevance) rather than a simple binary state of being either useful or useless.15

The rigorous computation of NDCG@10 is executed through a sequential, multi-stage mathematical process involving the calculation of Cumulative Gain (CG), Discounted Cumulative Gain (DCG), and Ideal Discounted Cumulative Gain (IDCG).13

The initial stage involves calculating the Cumulative Gain (CG). This metric measures the raw sum of the relevance scores for all documents retrieved within the top ![][image2] positions, intentionally ignoring their exact rank order. It provides a baseline assessment of the total utility retrieved by the system. The mathematical formulation for Cumulative Gain is defined as:

![][image11]  
In this equation, ![][image12] represents the predetermined, graded relevance score of the document located at position ![][image13] within the ranked list.15 Because Cumulative Gain treats a highly relevant document at rank 1 identically to a highly relevant document at rank 10, it must be subjected to a discounting function to properly evaluate ranking quality.

The subsequent stage involves computing the Discounted Cumulative Gain (DCG). This function applies a strict logarithmic penalty to the relevance score based entirely on the document's position in the ranking hierarchy. This logarithmic decay ensures that the retrieval system is heavily rewarded for placing the most relevant data assets at the very top of the list, while the contribution of relevant documents found further down the page is severely diminished.8 The standard, widely accepted formulation for DCG is:

![][image14]  
In specific enterprise scenarios that place an extreme premium on highly relevant documents over marginally relevant ones—such as ensuring a production financial table vastly outscores a deprecated staging table—an alternative, exponentiated formulation is frequently utilized by relevance engineers:

![][image15]  
Because DCG values are inherently unbounded and vary wildly depending on the total number of relevant documents available within the corpus for a specific query, the raw DCG score must be mathematically normalized to allow for cross-query averaging and comparative benchmarking across different retrieval algorithms.8 This normalization is achieved by calculating the Ideal DCG (IDCG). The IDCG represents the absolute maximum possible DCG achievable if the exact same set of retrieved documents were sorted perfectly by their ground-truth relevance scores in strict descending order.14

![][image16]  
Here, ![][image17] represents the list of relevant documents ordered perfectly by their true relevance up to position ![][image2]. Finally, the Normalized Discounted Cumulative Gain at ![][image2] (NDCG@k) is computed as the simple ratio of the actual DCG achieved by the system to the theoretically perfect IDCG:

![][image18]  
The resulting calculation yields a highly standardized score ranging between 0.0 and 1.0. A score of 1.0 mathematically guarantees that the retrieval system produced a perfectly optimal ranking of the available enterprise assets, maximizing user utility.13 The inherent capacity of NDCG to natively handle graded relevance makes it the ideal metric for enterprise metadata search. It allows the evaluation framework to assign nuanced, multi-tiered scores—for example, granting a score of 3 for the authoritative production table, a score of 2 for a highly related analytical dashboard, and a score of 1 for a deprecated legacy table that still contains matching keywords but should be avoided by modern business users.17

| Metric Characteristic | Hit Rate@k | NDCG@k |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Rank Awareness** | Systematically ignores rank order. | Heavily penalizes poor ranking via logarithmic discounting. |
| **Relevance Type** | Strictly binary (Relevant vs. Irrelevant). | Supports highly granular, graded relevance scales (e.g., 0-3). |
| **Primary Utility** | Diagnosing catastrophic retrieval failures and baseline corpus coverage. | Evaluating the precise quality of the sorting algorithm, embedding models, and rerankers. |
| **Value Range** | Bounded continuously from 0.0 to 1.0. | Bounded continuously from 0.0 to 1.0. |
| **Data Requirements** | Requires identification of at least one target asset. | Requires an exhaustive ranking of all relevant candidate assets. |

## **Architectural Constraints and the Ground Truth Deficit**

The fundamental bottleneck in reliably calculating NDCG@10 and Hit Rate@10 is the acquisition of a robust, statistically significant ground-truth dataset. In the context of information retrieval evaluation, a ground-truth dataset acts as a comprehensive, human-verified mapping of natural language user queries to their corresponding, relevance-graded data asset IDs.18 In mature, consumer-facing web search engines or large-scale e-commerce environments, these complex relevance judgments are extracted implicitly from millions of daily user interactions, including click-through rates, prolonged dwell times, add-to-cart events, and successful conversions.11

However, the specified enterprise environment presents a severe architectural constraint: extreme clickstream sparsity. Operating a low-volume internal search service means that the sheer number of queries executed by users is insufficient to train machine learning models or reliably evaluate system performance. Furthermore, the explicit tracking of clicks on the retrieved datasets is currently missing from the infrastructure, though a tracking pipeline is under development.4 Without historical interaction telemetry, traditional methods for bootstrapping relevance judgments are entirely impossible. The ground truth must therefore be synthesized and architected through alternative, highly engineered data pipelines.4

Compounding this telemetry deficit is the intricate nature of the enterprise metadata index itself. The assets being searched are not monolithic blocks of text. Instead, they are highly structured metadata representations of tables, databases, charts, dashboards, and metrics. A single asset payload might include a plain-text description, an array of column schemas, categorical tags, curated glossary terms, and vital operational signals such as algorithmic popularity scores and departmental team usage metrics.20 Any evaluation dataset constructed for this environment must be capable of understanding queries that reference these specific metadata fields, and the resulting ground truth must accurately reflect the operational reality that a highly utilized table is vastly more relevant than an abandoned table with an identical schema.

To overcome these severe architectural limitations, the proposed framework relies on a multifaceted, hybrid dataset generation strategy. First, it exploits the existing golden QnA dataset by programmatically converting natural language interactions into structured query-to-document relevance pairs using the existing BM25 index. Second, it aggressively expands the evaluation scope and mitigates the cold-start problem by leveraging Large Language Models to autonomously synthesize hundreds of highly realistic search queries directly from the raw enterprise metadata schemas. Finally, it utilizes LLMs as automated, impartial judges to assign the multi-graded relevance scores required for the NDCG calculation, seamlessly blending semantic textual matching with quantitative popularity and usage telemetry.

## **Transforming Golden QnA Datasets into Information Retrieval Benchmarks**

The presence of an existing golden dataset composed of natural language questions and answers provides a highly valuable, domain-specific foundation for evaluating the enterprise search system.23 These human-verified interactions perfectly encapsulate the actual vocabulary, acronyms, and intent structures utilized by the organization's data consumers. However, an immediate structural dilemma arises when attempting to utilize this data for search evaluation: QnA datasets are inherently designed for evaluating generative language tasks, such as reading comprehension or conversational summarization. They are formatted as abstract text-to-text pairs, whereas computing NDCG and Hit Rate absolutely requires text-to-asset ID mappings.19

Adapting this generative dataset into a strict information retrieval benchmark requires an automated data engineering pipeline that traces the linguistic answers back to the specific metadata objects (tables, dashboards, or metrics) from which those answers are derived. This reverse-engineering process is executed through a sequence of highly deterministic programmatic steps.

The initial phase of this transformation requires generating a pool of candidate assets from the enterprise index. Because the retrieval system currently utilizes a text-based index powered by the BM25 ranking algorithm, this existing infrastructure serves as an exceptionally strong baseline for candidate generation. BM25, an advanced probabilistic extension of the traditional TF-IDF (Term Frequency-Inverse Document Frequency) model, excels at exact-match keyword searches and is highly proficient in handling structured technical identifiers often found in database schemas.25

To execute the mapping, the pipeline treats the text of the golden *answer*—rather than the user's initial question—as the primary search query against the BM25 index.27 The logic dictates that the human-verified answer contains the precise terminology, column names, or metric definitions that directly describe the target asset. The BM25 algorithm processes this query by calculating term frequencies and inverse document frequencies across the various indexed fields, such as table descriptions, column names, and glossary tags. It subsequently returns a ranked list of candidate asset IDs that contain the specific vocabulary utilized in the golden answer.29

However, relying solely on BM25 candidate retrieval is insufficient for establishing a rigorous ground truth. In complex enterprise environments characterized by highly denormalized or duplicated data architectures, BM25 will frequently return multiple potential matches. A query regarding a specific financial metric might retrieve the production fact table, several departmental staging tables, and a downstream reporting dashboard, all of which share identical column names and metadata descriptions. To isolate the definitive, ground-truth asset required for Hit Rate evaluation, the pipeline must employ automated entity linking and advanced semantic disambiguation.30

An advanced Large Language Model is integrated into the orchestration pipeline to act as an intelligent entity resolver. The LLM is presented with the original natural language question, the human-verified golden answer, and the complete metadata payloads (schemas, descriptions, and tags) of the top ![][image19] candidate assets retrieved by the BM25 baseline. The model is explicitly prompted to analyze the schema context of each candidate to determine which specific asset possesses the structural capacity to logically answer the question.30

For example, if the golden QnA discusses "daily active users segmented by the EMEA region over the last quarter," the LLM will systematically analyze the schemas of the retrieved candidate tables. It will look for tables containing both temporal date columns and geographic region identifiers, while simultaneously filtering out tables that lack the necessary granularity. Once the LLM logically isolates the correct asset, the asset's unique identifier (such as a specific table URI or dashboard ID) is explicitly mapped to the original natural language question, successfully converting the QnA pair into a rigorous retrieval benchmark.

Information retrieval evaluation requires not only positive ground-truth labels but also a realistic distribution of negative examples to properly measure the system's ability to rank irrelevant items lower.33 Inspired by the Text REtrieval Conference (TREC) pooling methodology, the pipeline retains the entirety of the candidate pool generated during the BM25 retrieval phase.4 The specific asset identified through the LLM entity linking process is marked as highly relevant, while the remaining unlinked assets within the pool are retained for subsequent automated grading. This ensures that the evaluation dataset contains a diverse array of hard negatives—assets that share keyword overlap with the query but are semantically incorrect—which is vital for testing the true discriminatory power of the retrieval system.5

## **Synthetic Query Generation (SQG) from Enterprise Schemas**

While the adapted golden QnA dataset provides exceptionally high-fidelity, human-verified queries, it is typically insufficient in sheer volume to provide the statistical power necessary for comprehensive system evaluation. An enterprise data catalog may contain tens of thousands of tables, metrics, and dashboards, yet a curated QnA dataset might only cover a few hundred highly popular assets. To achieve the requisite scale, address the cold-start problem for newly ingested data assets, and ensure operational coverage across the entire long-tail of the catalog, the evaluation framework must heavily leverage Synthetic Query Generation (SQG).35

Synthetic Query Generation utilizes the immense generative capabilities and in-context reasoning properties of advanced LLMs to operate the retrieval paradigm in reverse: rather than retrieving a metadata document based on a user's query, the LLM autonomously generates a highly realistic user search query based on a predefined metadata document.35 This innovative approach guarantees that every generated query has a perfect, deterministic mapping to a specific ground-truth asset ID, bypassing the need for complex entity linking.37

The architecture for executing enterprise metadata SQG involves several highly controlled stages designed to prevent model hallucination, enforce strict schema adherence, and ensure maximum query diversity across different user intents.

The foundation of effective SQG lies in rigorous metadata preprocessing and subschema enumeration. LLMs perform optimally when grounded in rich, meticulously structured context. Raw technical metadata, such as raw Data Definition Language (DDL) statements or unstructured database dumps, must be preprocessed into LLM-digestible formats prior to prompt injection.35 The catalog metadata is systematically flattened into comprehensive JSON or YAML structures that cleanly encapsulate the table name, detailed column descriptions, primary and foreign key relationships, associated business glossary terms, and any relevant data governance classifications.39

To generate queries that accurately simulate real-world analytical tasks rather than simple keyword lookups, the system executes subschema enumeration.35 Rather than generating synthetic queries for a single database table in isolation, the pipeline algorithmically identifies logical clusters of connected tables. For instance, it might extract a central sales fact table alongside its linked dimensional lookup tables (e.g., date, customer, and product dimensions) via established foreign key relationships. These interconnected subschemas are injected directly into the LLM context, providing the model with the semantic landscape necessary to generate complex, multi-hop search queries that perfectly reflect actual business intelligence workflows.40

The prompt engineering strategy deployed for SQG is the most critical factor in avoiding homogeneous, artificially clean queries that fail to mimic erratic human search behavior.37 The system prompt must explicitly instruct the LLM to adopt specific organizational user personas. A data scientist exploring predictive features will formulate a drastically different search query than a marketing analyst searching for quarterly aggregate KPIs.35

An effective SQG prompt template incorporates a highly structured set of directives to govern the LLM's output. It begins with a strong role assertion, instructing the model to simulate an enterprise data consumer navigating an internal knowledge catalog. The prompt then injects the structured JSON representation of the target asset or enumerated subschema to provide factual grounding. Crucially, the prompt enforces strict lexical constraints, explicitly prohibiting the LLM from utilizing the exact technical table names or exact column headers in the generated query. This forces the model to rely on semantic descriptions, synonyms, and broader business terminology, perfectly simulating the vocabulary mismatch that plagues real-world enterprise search.40

Furthermore, the prompt mandates extreme intent variation. The LLM is required to generate a diverse array of query typologies for the same underlying asset. This includes generating exact-match lookups (e.g., "where is the customer churn dataset located?"), broad exploratory searches (e.g., "show me tables containing European demographic data and encrypted email addresses"), and complex analytical questions that imply aggregations or joins (e.g., "what is the schema required for calculating monthly recurring revenue by sales territory?").40

Because LLMs are inherently probabilistic text generators, the resulting synthetic queries must undergo rigorous validation and filtering protocols before being admitted into the final evaluation dataset. The pipeline implements automated semantic validation filters to ensure that every generated query is logically answerable strictly using the provided schema context.35 Queries that hallucinate non-existent columns, invent fictional business metrics, or request data outside the scope of the provided enterprise metadata index are automatically identified and pruned from the dataset. The surviving, highly validated synthetic queries are then paired immutably with the exact asset IDs used to generate them, establishing a massive, high-volume ground-truth mapping tailored specifically for NDCG and Hit Rate evaluation.43

## **The LLM-as-a-Judge Framework for Graded Relevance**

With a comprehensive dataset of search queries—comprising both human-adapted QnA pairs and synthetically generated exploratory searches—and their associated target asset IDs firmly established, the evaluation framework must confront a fundamental reality of information retrieval: enterprise search relevance is rarely a binary construct.14 A single natural language query executed against the BM25 index may yield a highly varied set of results. It might return the authoritative production table representing a perfect match, an outdated legacy table representing a partial match, and a reporting dashboard that is only tangentially related to the core topic. To calculate an accurate, mathematically sound NDCG@10 score, the evaluation pipeline requires multi-graded relevance judgments for every single query-document pair retrieved during the testing phase.8

Historically, acquiring these graded judgments required securing highly specialized domain experts to manually review thousands of technical schema mappings, a process that is prohibitively slow, exceptionally expensive, and impossible to scale.5 Consequently, the contemporary industry standard for resolving this evaluation bottleneck involves deploying an LLM-as-a-Judge architecture.48 This highly automated paradigm utilizes a frontier foundation model to assess the semantic alignment and operational utility of a retrieved asset against a specific query, guided strictly by a meticulously engineered grading rubric.49

The architectural implementation of the LLM judge operates by receiving a carefully orchestrated triad of inputs: the raw user search query, the structured metadata representation of the asset retrieved by the BM25 index, and the detailed evaluation instructions outlining the grading criteria.48 Instead of merely measuring vector proximity or basic lexical token overlap, the judge model performs deep semantic reasoning. It systematically assesses whether the specific columns, technical descriptions, and business tags of the retrieved table possess the informational capacity to fully resolve the user's underlying search intent.51

To ensure deterministic, machine-readable outputs suitable for automated pipeline ingestion and large-scale metric computation, the LLM judge is severely restricted via prompt engineering and API-level enforcement constraints, such as explicit function calling or strict JSON mode architectures.52 The mandated output payload contains the numerical score based on the rubric, alongside a mandatory "Chain-of-Thought" (CoT) rationale.48 Forcing the model to generate a detailed, textual justification before outputting the final numerical score significantly enhances the judge's reasoning capabilities, mitigates premature conclusions, and provides critical, human-readable auditability for search engineers diagnosing ranking algorithmic failures.55

The overarching reliability and accuracy of an LLM judge are entirely contingent upon the precision and clarity of its grading rubric.50 Vague, generalized instructions consistently produce high variance in scoring and demonstrate poor correlation with actual human preference. The rubric must explicitly decompose the abstract concept of "search relevance" into distinct, observable conditions tailored specifically to the unique nuances of data catalog architectures.50

The evaluation framework implements a standardized 4-point graded scale ranging from 0 to 3\. This specific numerical distribution aligns seamlessly with the requirements of the NDCG mathematical formulation, allowing for precise exponential discounting calculations.14

| Relevance Score | Categorical Definition | Evaluation Criteria for Enterprise Metadata Assets |
| :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **3** | **Perfect Match (Highly Relevant)** | The asset is the authoritative, definitive source for the query. The metadata schema completely satisfies the user's informational or analytical intent. Key business terms in the query are explicitly validated by the asset's tags, descriptions, and specific column structures. No further searching is required by the user. |
| **2** | **Relevant (Good Match)** | The asset contains the necessary information to address the query but may require additional joins to other tables to be fully utilized, or it represents a secondary but valid source (e.g., a downstream aggregated dashboard rather than the raw base table). The semantic overlap between the query intent and the asset description is strong. |
| **1** | **Marginally Relevant (Partial Match)** | The asset is topically related to the query but fundamentally insufficient to fully answer it. It may share significant keyword overlap or belong to the same broader business domain, but it distinctly lacks the specific columns, temporal scope, or data granularity explicitly requested by the user. |
| **0** | **Irrelevant (No Match)** | The asset has absolutely no semantic or operational relevance to the query. Any textual overlap is purely coincidental (e.g., matching common stop words or generic industry terms), and the data contained within the asset cannot possibly be used to fulfill the user's stated objective. |

While incredibly scalable, LLM judges are fundamentally susceptible to inherent algorithmic biases that can severely skew evaluation metrics if left unaddressed by the engineering team.59 The framework implements several robust defensive strategies to ensure judgment fidelity and maintain metric integrity.

The primary vulnerability is verbosity bias. LLMs exhibit a heavily documented psychological tendency to assign higher relevance scores to longer, more verbose documents, regardless of their actual factual utility.50 An extensively documented but entirely irrelevant legacy table might score higher than a sparsely documented but highly relevant production table. To combat this, the system prompt explicitly instructs the judge to heavily prioritize the exactness of schema matching and the presence of critical data columns over the sheer length of the table description.

Furthermore, LLM judges often suffer from domain knowledge gaps. For highly specialized enterprise environments, the judge may lack the internal corporate knowledge required to accurately assess proprietary acronyms or highly specific internal nomenclature. To mitigate this, the evaluation pipeline dynamically injects "grading notes" or an abbreviated enterprise data dictionary directly into the system prompt. This provides the judge with the highly localized, contextual grounding necessary to evaluate the query accurately.61

Finally, the system requires continuous calibration to maintain long-term trust in the automated metrics. A statistically significant, randomly selected subset of the LLM's judgments (typically around 5%) is routinely sampled and rigorously audited by human domain experts.50 The statistical correlation between the LLM's automated scores and the human baseline is continuously calculated using metrics such as Cohen's Kappa or Pearson correlation. If the alignment drifts below an acceptable threshold, the grading rubric is iteratively refined and prompt instructions are tightened.54

## **Synthesizing Popularity and Team Usage into Composite Relevance**

A critical, defining differentiator of enterprise metadata search compared to generalized web retrieval or standard document search is the profound importance of operational and behavioral signals.63 In an enterprise data lake containing thousands of tables, it is exceedingly common for multiple assets to possess nearly identical schemas and semantic descriptions. For example, a search for "monthly recurring revenue" may legitimately match the highly governed production financial table, a deprecated legacy table from a prior system migration, and three temporary staging tables created by individual data engineers for ad-hoc analysis.1

If evaluated purely on semantic textual relevance by an LLM judge, all five of these tables might receive a high relevance score (e.g., a 2 or 3\) because they all technically contain the exact columns required to calculate the metric. However, from an enterprise perspective, presenting a deprecated or staging table at the top of the search results constitutes a catastrophic failure in user experience and data governance. Therefore, the ground-truth relevance label used to calculate NDCG@10 must be mathematically adjusted to incorporate the asset's popularity score and team usage metrics.7

To derive the final ground-truth label, the framework establishes a composite relevance function. This function seamlessly blends the qualitative, semantic judgment provided by the LLM with the quantitative, empirical telemetry extracted directly from the data catalog platform.12 This composite approach ensures that the evaluation metrics mathematically reward the retrieval system for surfacing assets that are not only textually accurate but also operationally authoritative and widely trusted by the organization.

The integration is achieved by meticulously normalizing the disparate operational metrics and applying them as scaling factors or direct modifiers to the base semantic score.

The metadata index contains raw operational signals, such as a general "popularity score" and "team usage" statistics. Popularity scores are often calculated via complex PageRank-style algorithms based on data lineage, downstream dashboard dependencies, and total query volume.20 Team usage indicates the frequency with which specific roles or departments interact with the asset. Because these raw values exhibit extreme variance and often follow steep power-law distributions (where a few core tables have millions of queries and most tables have near zero), they cannot be applied directly. They must be subjected to logarithmic smoothing and strict min-max normalization, producing a continuous usage multiplier (![][image20]) bounded strictly between ![][image21] and ![][image22].

![][image23]  
Once normalized, the composite relevance score (![][image24]) is calculated. This composite score serves as the final ground-truth label ![][image12] that is inserted directly into the core DCG and IDCG mathematical equations. It is formulated as a weighted combination of the semantic score (![][image25]) provided by the LLM judge and the normalized usage multiplier (![][image20]).

![][image26]  
In this formulation:

* ![][image27] is the rubric-based score assigned by the LLM-as-a-Judge.  
* $U\_m \\in $ is the normalized popularity/usage signal.  
* ![][image28] and ![][image29] are hyperparameter weights determined by the organization's specific optimization goals (e.g., ![][image30] places primary emphasis on semantic meaning, while ![][image31] applies a substantial boost for authoritative usage).

Crucially, the multiplication of ![][image25] and ![][image20] in the second term acts as a vital algorithmic safeguard. It ensures that astronomical popularity cannot mathematically salvage a semantically irrelevant document. If the LLM judge determines a highly popular table has zero semantic relevance to the user's specific query (![][image32]), the composite score remains exactly zero, regardless of how frequently the table is used globally. Conversely, among a cohort of semantically identical tables that all receive a perfect semantic score (![][image33]), the composite function effectively acts as a highly sensitive tie-breaker, mathematically elevating the highly utilized production table above the abandoned staging tables.

This formulation yields a highly nuanced, continuous relevance spectrum (e.g., a score of 3.25 for a highly relevant, highly popular asset versus a 2.1 for a highly relevant but rarely used asset). Because NDCG mathematical formulations are inherently designed to accommodate any continuous positive scale, these composite scores can be ingested directly into the NDCG@10 calculation without requiring any structural modification to the underlying metric.14

## **Orchestrating the Continuous Evaluation Pipeline**

The theoretical foundations of metric formulation and dataset generation must be operationalized within a robust, automated, and repeatable infrastructure. Evaluating search quality is not a static, one-time exercise but rather a continuous lifecycle critical to the maintenance and evolution of enterprise knowledge systems.68 The orchestration of this pipeline ensures that any modifications to the retrieval architecture—whether fine-tuning the BM25 BM25 ![][image34] and ![][image35] parameters, implementing new dense vector embeddings, or deploying advanced cross-encoder rerankers—are instantly validated against a stable, comprehensive benchmark.69

The operational architecture executes in a highly structured, iterative loop designed for continuous integration. First, the pipeline manages Dataset Construction and Versioning. The system periodically ingests the latest corporate metadata schemas and existing golden QnA pairs. It executes the BM25 candidate pooling and entity-linking protocols to align human queries with specific asset IDs.10 Concurrently, the SQG module generates massive batches of synthetic queries reflecting new subschemas or emerging data domains to ensure the dataset remains fresh and representative.43 This unified dataset is rigorously version-controlled. Versioning is absolutely paramount to ensure that longitudinal fluctuations in NDCG and Hit Rate represent actual algorithmic changes in the search engine rather than silent shifts in the underlying test data.68

Following dataset construction, the pipeline initiates Simulation and Retrieval Execution. For a given evaluation run, the core search system is treated entirely as a black box. The evaluation harness systematically issues the hundreds of synthesized and human-curated queries against the live or mirrored retrieval infrastructure. It meticulously captures the top 10 ranked assets for each query, strictly logging the order alongside critical system latency metrics.70

The retrieved results are then dispatched to the Asynchronous Automated Grading phase. The retrieved asset metadata is passed to the LLM-as-a-Judge microservice. Operating strictly on the explicitly defined 0-3 relevance rubric and injected with the necessary grading notes, the LLM analyzes each query-document pair in total isolation, returning structured semantic relevance scores and detailed analytical rationales.55

Finally, the pipeline executes Signal Blending and Metric Computation. The system intercepts the raw LLM semantic scores and joins them with the operational usage and popularity metadata extracted from the catalog's telemetry systems. The composite relevance function is applied to generate the final, nuanced ground-truth labels. The evaluation framework then executes the mathematical formulations for Hit Rate@10 and NDCG@10, aggregating the results across the entire query set to produce the final system score.10

The final outputs are visualized in Analytics and Diagnostics Dashboards to provide search engineering teams with immediate, actionable insights. Beyond aggregate scores, the pipeline isolates specific queries exhibiting severe NDCG degradation, leveraging the LLM judge's CoT rationales to perform automated root-cause analysis.73 This granular visibility allows engineers to discern exactly why the system failed. They can determine whether failures are caused by basic lexical mismatches (requiring BM25 synonym expansion or analyzer tuning), deep semantic misunderstandings (requiring better embedding models or query expansion), or popularity bias overrides (requiring delicate adjustments to the hybrid ![][image28] and ![][image29] ranking algorithms).74

The evaluation of search quality over complex enterprise metadata indices represents a critical frontier in modern data architecture. In the absolute absence of massive clickstream data, relying on intuition or rudimentary exact-match evaluations fundamentally obscures the genuine utility of the data catalog and severely degrades organizational data fluency. By orchestrating a sophisticated evaluation ecosystem—leveraging adapted golden datasets, expansive synthetic query generation, and strict rubric-driven automated LLM judgments—organizations can mathematically quantify their system's true performance.

The utilization of Hit Rate@10 ensures that the baseline retrieval infrastructure successfully surfaces necessary documents, while the application of NDCG@10 provides a highly sensitive, rank-aware measurement of the sorting algorithm's precise efficacy. Most importantly, by algorithmically blending semantic LLM assessments with empirical usage and popularity metadata, the resulting ground-truth dataset accurately mirrors the intricate realities of enterprise data consumption. This rigorous, quantifiable approach ultimately empowers data teams to iteratively deploy advanced retrieval and reranking models with absolute confidence, transforming static data catalogs into highly intelligent, predictive engines of corporate discovery.

#### **Works cited**

1. Improving Enterprise Data Product Discoverability through AI-Driven Metadata Enrichment \- TechRxiv, accessed May 12, 2026, [https://www.techrxiv.org/doi/pdf/10.36227/techrxiv.177078341.17395557](https://www.techrxiv.org/doi/pdf/10.36227/techrxiv.177078341.17395557)
